title: [source] A quantity's public label is its entity ID, not the value stored in its `label`

given: A quantity has ID `42`.
Its stored `label` field is `Harvest weight`.
when: A consumer requests the quantity label.
then: The observable label is `42`, not `Harvest weight`.

why_non_obvious: The entity declares `label` as its label key and also has a user-facing Label field, so a conventional implementation would return that field. The explicit `label()` override instead exposes the ID.

source_read.rule: A quantity's public label is its entity ID, not the value stored in its `label` field.

title: [source] When a quantity is deleted directly, every log that references it has all matchi

given: A log references a quantity, potentially more than once.
when: That quantity is deleted.
then: All of that quantity's references are removed from every referencing log, and each affected log is saved as a new revision with a removal message.

why_non_obvious: The inverse cleanup is not a simple dangling-reference cleanup: it affects every referencing log, removes every matching item, and creates an auditable revision.

source_read.rule: When a quantity is deleted directly, every log that references it has all matching references removed and is saved as a new revision with a removal message.

title: [source] Cloning a log replaces every referenced quantity with a newly duplicated quantit

given: A log references one or more quantities.
when: The log clone event is handled.
then: The cloned log's quantity references point to duplicates of each original quantity, not the original quantity entities.

why_non_obvious: A log clone can appear to imply copying only the log/reference list; this explicitly gives each cloned log independent quantity records.

source_read.rule: Cloning a log replaces every referenced quantity with a newly duplicated quantity, rather than retaining references to the original quantity entities.

title: [source] Assets selected by the action are eligible for the redirected log form only if t

given: An asset was selected while the action was available because the user had update access.
Before confirmation, the user lacks view access to that asset.
At least one other selected asset remains viewable.
when: The user confirms a log type.
then: The add-log redirect contains only the viewable asset IDs; the non-viewable asset is omitted.

why_non_obvious: A port may consistently use the action’s update permission, or assume selection-time authorization is sufficient. The source applies a different, later view-access filter before constructing the asset query parameter.

source_read.rule: On an add-log form, an `asset` query parameter ultimately sets the log’s asset field to all raw supplied IDs—including IDs that were not loaded or failed view access—despite an earlier loop that appears to append only viewable assets.

title: [source] Migration creates and saves one quantity per item only when the input is an arra

given: A migration invokes `create_quantity` with a scalar source value.
when: The process transform runs.
then: It returns an empty result and creates no quantity.

why_non_obvious: The process plugin advertises `handle_multiples`, and its name suggests it can create a quantity from an input value. Its implementation explicitly skips all non-iterable input without an error.

source_read.rule: Migration creates and saves one quantity per item only when the input is an array or traversable; scalar input silently produces no quantities.

title: [source] `plain_text` quantity rendering decodes HTML entities, strips tags, trims edge w

given:
A quantity's rendered output is ` A&B
\n C `.
when: It is viewed in `plain_text` mode.
then: The final rendered text is `A&B C`.

why_non_obvious: “Plain text” can be implemented as only tag removal or escaping. This renderer additionally decodes entities first and normalizes all internal whitespace.

source_read.rule: `plain_text` quantity rendering decodes HTML entities, strips tags, trims edge whitespace, and collapses every whitespace run to one space.

title: [source] Deleting a log deletes all quantity entities it references, even if those quanti

given: Log A and Log B both reference quantity Q.
when: Log A is deleted.
then: Quantity Q is deleted, despite Log B also referencing it.

why_non_obvious: A reimplementation might treat referenced quantities as shared data and only remove the deleted log's links; this hook deletes the target entities themselves.

source_read.rule: Deleting a log deletes all quantity entities it references, even if those quantities are also referenced by other logs.

title: [source] Migration validates each newly assembled quantity before saving; any violation t

given: A migrated item assembles a quantity with an invalid field value.
when: `create_quantity` processes that item.
then: No ID is allocated through save for that invalid quantity, and a `MigrateSkipRowException` is raised with the violation path and message.

why_non_obvious: An event-log port might accept invalid events for later reconciliation, or save a partial entity and report validation separately. This plugin makes validation a pre-save gate and turns violations into row skipping.

source_read.rule: Migration validates each newly assembled quantity before saving; any violation throws a skip-row exception, so an invalid item is not saved and the migration row is skipped.

title: [source] The quantity field is a multi-valued revision-aware entity reference, so a log s

given: A log has multiple quantity measurements.
A quantity may have revisions.
when: Quantity references are stored on the log.
then: The log can retain multiple revision-aware references to quantity entities.

why_non_obvious: A port based on the field name may model a single ordinary foreign-key reference and lose multiplicity and revision-specific linkage.

source_read.rule: The quantity field is a multi-valued revision-aware entity reference, so a log stores references to quantity revisions rather than only stable quantity identities.

title: [source] Executing “Add a log referencing assets” does not create or append anything; it

given: User U has previously invoked the action for assets A and B.
User U subsequently invokes the action for asset C.
when: The confirmation form is opened for User U.
then: The pending selection is C only; A and B are not retained or merged.

why_non_obvious: An action named “Add a log” can be mistaken for an immediate additive command, but it only stages a per-user confirmation payload, and a later invocation overwrites the earlier payload.

source_read.rule: The pending add-log selection is consumed on every confirmation-form submission, including cancellation, missing confirmation, or a path that produces no redirect asset list.

title: [source] For each migrated item, configured field values preferentially come from that it

given: An item contains `amount: 3`.
The enclosing migration row contains `amount: 9`.
The plugin maps `value/numerator` from `amount`.
when: The item is transformed.
then: The created quantity receives numerator `3`; `9` is only a fallback if the item has no non-null `amount`.

why_non_obvious: A port might always read configured properties from the outer migration row, or merge item and outer values differently. The null-coalescing lookup gives item-level values precedence and writes to nested paths.

source_read.rule: For each migrated item, configured field values preferentially come from that item's own row; the enclosing source row is used only when the item value is null or absent, and nested destination paths are supported.

title: [source] Quantity values are revisioned records: `measure`, fractional `value`, units, la

given: A quantity exists with a measure and fractional value.
A later edit changes its value or units.
when: The edit is persisted as a revision according to the quantity type's revision policy.
then: The resulting state must retain revision-specific versions of those fields, even though no revision UI is shown.

why_non_obvious: A hidden revision UI can make the entity appear non-revisioned, but the entity base class and field definitions preserve revision history; treating updates as only an in-place latest-value replacement loses source semantics.

source_read.rule: Units may reference only taxonomy terms in the `unit` bundle, but an unknown unit may be created automatically as a new `unit` term.

title: [source] A log may reference multiple assets; the asset reference is not single-valued.

given: A user selects multiple accessible assets and chooses a log type.
when: The add-log flow redirects to a new log form.
then: All selected asset IDs are carried as an asset collection, and the log field supports multiple references.

why_non_obvious: A port modeled around a singular “asset” relationship could retain only one selected asset, while bulk add-log redirects and the base field both support a collection.

source_read.rule: A log may reference multiple assets; the asset reference is not single-valued.

title: [source] Default quantity type selection is ordered: a non-empty per-log-type setting win

given: A log type has no non-empty configured default quantity type.
The farm_quantity_standard module is installed.
Other quantity types may exist.
when: The default quantity type is requested.
then: The result is "standard", rather than another available quantity type.

why_non_obvious: The method name suggests a straightforward configured default, but it has module-dependent fallback behavior and an order-dependent arbitrary-type fallback.

source_read.rule: The form applies a computed default quantity type only when that type is present among the currently selectable bundle options.

title: [cm/access-check] access

given: an actor and a resource with a server-side access rule
when: the resource is read/written under the eventual, selective-disclosure model
then: the value visible to the actor matches the source's access decision

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this access-check site 'soft': [access-check] The source assumes an update authorization decision from `$object->access(...)`; under eventual consistency, stale permission/object state can transiently allow or deny access, so the check is an access snapshot that can converge rather than an inherently consistency-model-dependent invariant.

title: [cm/access-check] entityPrepareForm

given: an actor and a resource with a server-side access rule
when: the resource is read/written under the eventual, selective-disclosure model
then: the value visible to the actor matches the source's access decision

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this access-check site 'soft': [access-check] The source assumes a current, authoritative `$asset->access('view', $this->currentUser)` decision; under eventual consistency a replicated permission or revocation may be stale and temporarily allow or deny viewing, so access reflects an eventually consistent authorization snapshot.

title: [cm/revision-lock] QuantityType

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] The `new_revision` marker indicates revision history rather than an invariant requiring a single-writer lock, so concurrent revisions may temporarily coexist and converge or remain as history under eventual consistency.

title: [cm/access-check] submitForm

given: an actor and a resource with a server-side access rule
when: the resource is read/written under the eventual, selective-disclosure model
then: the value visible to the actor matches the source's access decision

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this access-check site 'soft': [access-check] The source assumes a current-user view permission check is authoritative at submission time; under eventual consistency, replicated permission or entity state may briefly be stale and yield a transiently different access decision, so the invariant can hold eventually.

title: [cm/revision-lock] Quantity

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] The `revision` mapping to `revision_id` records revision state/history, which may be temporarily stale or divergent during concurrent offline updates but can converge once revisions synchronize; it does not by itself establish an unconditionally exclusive lock.

title: [cm/revision-lock] baseFieldDefinitions

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] `setRevisionable(TRUE)` assumes revision history for the entity; concurrent local revisions may temporarily diverge or have competing ancestry, but the revision history can converge eventually without requiring every write to be coordinated.

title: [cm/revision-lock] quantityDelete

given: a record two writers update from the same base revision
when: both updates are applied without a central lock
then: the converged value and the fate of the lost update are asserted

why_non_obvious: intent-CM graded this revision-lock site 'soft': [revision-lock] `$log->setNewRevision(TRUE)` assumes each change is recorded as a new revision; under eventual consistency replicas may temporarily miss or order revisions differently, but the revision history can converge.

title: [domain-logic] QuantityInterface

given: the domain state this class operates on
when: its primary operation is exercised
then: the value it computes / maintains is observed at the boundary

why_non_obvious: A high-reach domain class concentrates behavior many members depend on; its non-obvious rules are load-bearing for a faithful port.

title: [hook-implementation] ThemeHooks

given: the entity state the hook keys on
when: the lifecycle event that triggers the hook occurs
then: the hook's derived side effect is observable at the value boundary

why_non_obvious: A hook fires a side effect on an entity lifecycle event. A re-implementer who models only the primary write can miss the derived state the hook maintains.

title: [views-filter] QuantityViewsData

given: records that the filter would include and exclude
when: the filtered read is taken
then: only the included records are returned, in the filter's order

why_non_obvious: A Views query/filter shapes what a read returns (visibility, ordering, exclusion). The filter semantics are invisible from the read's method name.
